items.question: What is the difference between let, const and var?
items.answer: `var` is function-scoped and hoisted (initialized as undefined). `let` and `const` are block-scoped and live in the temporal dead zone until declared. `const` additionally forbids reassignment of the binding (though object contents can still mutate).

items.question: Why does typeof null return 'object'?
items.answer: It's a long-standing bug from the first JavaScript implementation where values were tagged by type and the null pointer (0x00) shared the object tag. It was never fixed to preserve backward compatibility.

items.question: Explain the difference between primitive and reference types.
items.answer: Primitives (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol) are immutable and copied by value. Reference types (objects, arrays, functions) are stored on the heap and variables hold a reference, so assignment and passing copy the reference, not the value.
